notes: The Fool's Journey — the 22 arcana in canonical order as one run. Each entry: arcana id, canonical location, the SIGNATURE scenario the campaign launches (other setups stay in free play), act (1: 0-VII, 2: VIII-XIV, 3: XV-XXI), thread_award (insight earned on a win; act finales give more), and dean_beat (the escalating antagonist's interstitial line). See lore/_MAJOR_ARCANA_CAMPAIGN_DESIGN.md.

arcana.dean_beat: You do not know his name yet. He is a line in someone else's ledger, two tables over, already paid.
A commission comes through a broker you never met. The signature is initials. D.D.
Something watches the show under a handle that is only a dash. It comments once, then deletes it.
A reservation for Table 14 you did not take. Plain shoes. He tips exactly, and leaves before dessert.
The supplier mentions, unprompted, that a Mr. Dean has been asking after the boat's books.
The long black car idles a block from every stop on the circuit. Nineteen years, the same driver.
An older man in a charcoal suit stands at the chain-link, smoothing his lapels, waiting for a decision that is not his.
The wreck was arranged. You can't prove it. The estimate has his firm's letterhead. Act one closes; he has your address now.
Dean makes the auctioneer an offer for the whole lot. The lion is not for sale. He smiles as if it were.
A voice on channel 16 you do not recognize asks the keeper to confirm the light's schedule. It is not the Coast Guard.
Dean owns a piece of the house now. The wheel still turns fair. That is the frightening part.
His counsel is already seated when you arrive. Boudreaux. He has represented the other side on every Graustark matter for a decade.
The lease on the empty apartment transfers to a holding company. You know the initials before you read them.
Dean funds the ward's closing 'transition.' He is generous the way a tide is generous.
He buys the bar's building, keeps the bar. Sits at the W-stool once. Says 'evening.' Act two closes; he is at your table now.
The empty stool is not empty. It has not been empty for some time. Dean sits beside the thing he serves and orders for you both.
The tower's signal carries a second voice under the broadcast. It has been counting. It reaches your number.
Even here, the kindest of the readings, his ledger sits open on the loading dock. He is not cruel. He is thorough.
Between the feature and the static, his face in the projector beam for one frame. You cannot un-see the frame.
He walks the garden path at ten a.m. He compliments the roses. He asks, gently, whether you have thought about what comes after.
At the reading of the hard names, one name is added in a hand you recognize. He is asking you to say it aloud.
The loop closes. Across the felt, the thing that wore Dean the whole way sets down the last card. The reading is complete. So are you — one way, or the other.
